summary: Market sentiment remains broadly optimistic as Wall Street is on track for its strongest quarter in years, with risk appetite holding firm even amid the backdrop of the U.S.-Iran conflict, suggesting equity markets have largely priced in the geopolitical shock. The key macro drivers include ongoing U.S.-Iran diplomatic uncertainty, with envoy Witkoff and Kushner present in Qatar but no direct meeting with Iran confirmed, while the IMF reaching a staff-level deal with Egypt worth $1.6 billion adds a modest positive signal for emerging market stability. On the sector and stock front, oil markets are a focal point, with crude hovering just above $70 and set for its steepest monthly and quarterly loss since 2020, pressured by UAE exporting record oil volumes following its OPEC exit and tepid Asian import demand; meanwhile, at least one high-flying equity position is being trimmed after more than tripling in value, signaling some profit-taking in parabolic movers. The primary risks investors are monitoring include the fragile state of U.S.-Iran nuclear talks and the potential for escalation — underscored by the killing of two Revolutionary Guards members in western Iran — alongside the continued softness in oil prices threatening energy sector earnings and biofuel policy stability in emerging markets. In the short term, equities appear positioned to close the quarter on a strong note, but oil price weakness, unresolved Middle East diplomacy, and selective profit-taking suggest investors will approach Q3 with cautious, selective positioning rather than broad-based momentum.
